The Device

Final assembled device — 3D-printed PLA housing, 16×2 LCD displaying Ready to Count · Target: 10, KY-040 rotary encoder knob

Overview

Low-cost embedded automatic pill counter built for small and independent pharmacies. A rotating platform with passive mechanical guides creates a single-file stream of size 00 capsules, counted via an Adafruit IR break-beam sensor as they exit. The STM32H753ZI Nucleo governs all subsystems through a non-blocking FSM, with a 16×2 I²C LCD and rotary encoder providing the full user interface.

Achieved 100% counting accuracy across 30 independent test runs spanning 25, 50, and 100 pill targets.

Mechanical Design

A stepper motor drives the rotating platform at approximately 30 RPM. Stationary guides direct capsules radially outward toward a dispensing slot where a final guide enforces single-file alignment — it is slightly wider than one capsule but narrower than two, so any double-entry deflects the inner pill back onto the platform to re-sort on the next rotation. All structural parts were 3D-printed from PLA for rapid iteration.

MotorNEMA 17 · ~30 RPM
Capsule SizeSize 00
StructurePLA 3D-printed · 5 parts
AlignmentPassive single-file via guides
Hardware Architecture

A 12V 2A adapter powers the NEMA 17 and L298N driver; the driver's onboard 5V regulator feeds the Nucleo and all low-voltage peripherals, replacing the buck converter in the original proposal.

Hardware block diagram — MCU, power, actuation, sensing, and UI subsystems with pin annotations

Bill of Materials
ComponentPart / Notes
MicrocontrollerSTM32H753ZI Nucleo
Stepper MotorNEMA 17 · 4.1V · 1.8° step
Motor DriverL298N H-bridge · onboard 5V rail
IR SensorAdafruit Break-Beam Sensor
LCD Display16×2 I²C Module (PCF8574)
Rotary EncoderKY-040 360° with push button
Power Supply12V 2A AC/DC Adapter
Pin Configuration
PeripheralInterfaceMCU Pin(s)
IR Break-BeamEXTI falling edge · priority 1D2 → PG14
I²C LCDI²C SDA / SCLD14/D15 → PB9/PB8
Stepper Motor4× GPIO OutputD4–D8 → PE14 PE11 PG12 PF3
Encoder CLK/DTTIM3 quadrature encoder modeD11/D12 → PB5/PA6
Encoder SWEXTI · priority 2D13 → PA5
Firmware Architecture

Written in C using STM32CubeIDE with STM32 HAL throughout. A non-blocking superloop driven by a deterministic finite state machine — no RTOS, minimal overhead, ISRs trigger with zero delay.

Finite State Machine — four states, two operating modes, universal 10-second auto-timeout

Software Modules
SystemConfig.c — FSM

Manages all state transitions and both operating modes. The 10-second HAL_GetTick() timeout acts as a universal safety fallback — if no pill is detected for 10 s the FSM forces STATE_COMPLETE regardless of mode.

BeamSensorHandler.c — Counting

IR break-beam on EXTI falling-edge with 75 ms software debounce (tuned through physical testing). ISR only increments in STATE_RUNNING to prevent stray counts during idle or completion.

MotorDriver.c — Actuation

Hardcoded 4-step bipolar sequence — required because the L298N doesn't accept standard step/dir signals. Speed controlled at one step per 10 ms via HAL_GetTick(). Motor halts instantly on FSM exit from STATE_RUNNING.

UserInterface.c — LCD + Encoder

TIM3 in hardware quadrature encoder mode — no software polling, no missed clicks. Custom 4-bit I²C LCD driver sends commands as two nibbles. Variable snapshotting prevents LCD race conditions with the sensor ISR.

Debugging & Validation
I²C Bus Failures
STM32 internal pull-ups (~40 kΩ) caused rise-time violations, producing repeated HAL_I2C_Master_Transmit() failures. Replaced with external 4.7 kΩ resistors to 3.3V — all faults eliminated.
IR Sensor Miss Rate (~1-in-8)
Emitter divergence created a beam that small pills only partially obstructed. Applied black tape with a ~2 mm punched pinhole over the receiver window, restricting the beam to below the narrowest pill diameter. Miss rate dropped to zero.
Double-Counting
Irregular pill surfaces produced multiple falling edges per pass. Increased IR debounce from 20 ms to 75 ms and encoder button debounce from 20 ms to 350 ms — both thresholds determined through physical testing.

100% counting accuracy across 30 independent runs at 25, 50, and 100 pill targets. Inventory mode auto-timeout validated in all 5 trials.

Team
Ahmed Fahmi Anthony Sergnese Joy Mehany Lucas McMaster

MIE438 — Embedded Systems · University of Toronto · Group 12 · Jan–Apr 2026

The Platform

TurtleBot 4 — ROS 2 Robotics from Day One

MIE443 (Mechatronics Systems: Design and Integration) uses the TurtleBot 4 as its platform: a Clearpath Robotics robot built on the iRobot Create 3 differential-drive base, carrying an RPLIDAR A1M8 360-degree 2D LiDAR, OAK-D Lite stereo depth camera, multi-zone bumper, wheel encoders, IMU, and optical odometry. All software runs on ROS 2 (Humble) in C++. This project was completed as a team of four: Nicolas Rebollo Canedo-Arguelles, Ahmed Fahmi, Lucas McMaster, and Bido Mohamed.

Contest 1 — Autonomous SLAM Exploration

Where Am I? Map an Unknown Environment in 8 Minutes

The TurtleBot 4 is placed in an unknown 4.87 × 4.87 m environment with random walls and obstacles. It must autonomously map as much as possible within 8 minutes with no prior knowledge of the layout, no human intervention, and a speed cap of 0.25 m/s (0.10 m/s within 0.4 m of obstacles). Scoring is based on percentage of environment mapped versus a ground truth, plus accurate identification of key obstacles.

Sensor Design. The primary sensor is the 2D LiDAR, which provides 360-degree planar range measurements at 0.56-degree angular resolution. Scan data is split into three 40-degree directional windows (front, left, right). Invalid readings are explicitly filtered; the minimum per window captures worst-case obstacle proximity. The multi-zone bumper acts as a physical failsafe for obstacles the LiDAR misses: low-profile objects below the scan plane or narrow obstacles lost in noise. Wheel encoders and optical odometry together provide pose estimation (x, y, theta) for closed-loop motion control.

Control Architecture. A purely sequential controller was ruled out as the maze layout is unknown. A fully deliberative planner was too slow. A purely reactive controller is collision-safe but leads to directionless exploration and repeated traversal. The implemented controller is a hybrid reactive behaviour-based priority architecture: safety reactions dominate when needed, but state-dependent logic improves coverage efficiency.

The high-level controller is a 6-state Finite State Machine running at 10 Hz: (1) complete any ongoing motion; (2) complete any ongoing rotation; (3) bumper collision recovery; (4) side avoidance for obstacles within 0.2 m; (5) exploration reorientation when front is blocked at 0.3 m; (6) default forward motion. Internal state flags (moving_ and turning_) lock each manoeuvre until completion, preventing jitter.

Obstacle Avoidance Primitives. Side avoidance triggers a 15-degree corrective rotation away from the closer side when left or right FOV drops below 0.2 m. Front avoidance triggers the exploration algorithm when front FOV drops below 0.3 m. The bumper failsafe handles obstacles missed by LiDAR: reverse 0.15 m and re-run the exploration algorithm.

Exploration Algorithm — 4:1 Biased Narrow-Corridor Seeker. When the front is blocked, the full 360-degree scan is binarized: each beam becomes 1 (open) or 0 (close) relative to a dynamic threshold tied to the current maximum range. midSequenceFar() finds the longest run of open beams and returns the heading toward the widest open space. midSequenceClose() finds the shortest traversable gap (arc length larger than the TB4 diameter) and returns the heading toward the narrowest corridor. A 4:1 bias selects narrow corridors four out of every five reorientations: wide areas fall within LiDAR range and are mapped without the robot entering them; narrow corridors must be entered to be mapped. A 120-degree backward exclusion zone prevents doubling back.

Closed-Loop Motion Primitives. NormAngle() wraps yaw difference into [-pi, pi] for stable heading tracking. The turning_ state locks until the target heading change completes; the moving_ state locks until the Euclidean odometry distance equals the target displacement.

Contest 1 — Results and Future Work

What Worked and What Comes Next

The algorithm successfully navigated and mapped unknown environments within the 8-minute time limit. The 4:1 narrow-corridor bias drove the robot into occluded regions a pure open-space strategy would have missed. The 6-state FSM prevented oscillation and handled every collision failure mode encountered during testing. The primary limitation was the absence of spatial memory: the robot had no awareness of already-mapped regions, meaning it could revisit explored areas rather than redirecting effort toward genuinely unknown space.

The recommended next step is frontier-based exploration: identify the boundaries between known free space and unknown territory on the live SLAM occupancy grid, then use A* to navigate directly to the highest-value frontier. This converts the algorithm from reactive-with-bias to fully deliberate coverage.

Contest 2 — Pick and Place (In Progress)

I Can Help You Clean: Visual Object Recognition and Robotic Arm Manipulation

Contest 2 is currently under active development. The TurtleBot 4, fitted with a 5-DOF robotic arm, must detect a manipulable object on its top plate using YOLOv8, navigate to five scene objects in the environment, classify each using YOLOv8 on the OAK-D Lite camera, match the manipulable object to the correct scene object, localize the corresponding garbage bin via an AprilTag marker, deposit the object using the arm, and return to start — all within 5 minutes. The pipeline integrates ROS 2 Nav2 for map-based navigation, YOLOv8 for object detection, AprilTag for 3D bin pose estimation, and MoveIt 2 for arm motion planning. This contest will be updated upon completion.

The Mission

Build a Fully Autonomous Robot from Scratch — in One Semester

MIE444 (Mechatronics) is universally regarded as one of the most demanding courses in UofT MechE. The brief sounds deceptively simple: design, build, and program a robot called Tiny Tim that can autonomously navigate a physical maze, localize itself without GPS or encoders, pick up a block, and deliver it to one of four preprogrammed drop-off zones. Everything — chassis, gripper, wiring, firmware, localization algorithm, path planner — had to be built from zero in a single semester by a four-person team. What followed was a full semester of late nights, hardware failures, software rewrites, and subsystem-by-subsystem iteration that tested every engineering discipline simultaneously.

Part 1 — CAD and Physical Design

Building a Robot That Could Move in Any Direction

The core design decision was to use omni-directional wheels, which allow the robot to translate laterally in any direction without rotating. This was essential for navigating a tight maze where the robot could not always point toward its next destination. The chassis was built as a hybrid of laser-cut plywood structural panels — which provided rigidity and fast iteration — and 3D-printed elements that required precise geometry: the gripper jaws, the sensor mounts, and the dome shell. The circular body profile was a deliberate choice: rectangular robots accumulate collision risk at their corners in tight maze corridors. A rounded shell reduced the effective swept volume and eliminated blind spots at oblique angles.

The gripper was one of the most mechanically demanding sub-assemblies. It used a 2-DOF rack-and-pinion mechanism: the first degree of freedom deployed the gripper arm outward from the chassis, and the second closed the jaws around the block. Rubberized tape on the jaws provided friction, and a mechanical limit switch inside the chassis acted as a fail-safe sensor — if the switch was not pressed after the gripper was retracted, the robot knew the block had not been captured and would retry. All gripper geometry was 3D-printed and iterated multiple times across the semester as failure modes were discovered during testing.

Part 2 — Software and Autonomy

Three Rewrites, Two Localization Algorithms, One Working Robot

The software stack was the most complex and most rewritten part of the project. The architecture ran across two processors: an Arduino Mega 2560 handled all real-time low-level behaviour — motor commands, ToF sensor polling, gripper actuation, obstacle avoidance — while a laptop running Python handled the computationally expensive work: localization, path planning, and mission state management. The two communicated over Bluetooth via an ESP32 module. All source code is available at github.com/hossainrafiu/MIE444-Localization-Code.

Obstacle Avoidance — from 8 Ultrasonics to 4 ToF Sensors. The first iteration used 8 ultrasonic sensors — 4 in the primary travel directions and 4 in the diagonal directions to detect corners. In simulation it worked. On the physical robot, it failed: ultrasonic waves bounce unpredictably at large angles, causing the corner sensors to give wildly inconsistent readings, and the measurement latency was too long for the control loop. The team scrapped all 8 ultrasonics and replaced the 4 primary-direction sensors with Time-of-Flight sensors, which use laser light and are substantially faster and more precise. The final obstacle avoidance strategy was simple and reliable: continuously monitor left, right, and front ToF readings; if any reading drops below threshold, laterally translate away from the wall with a corrective rotation.

Localization — Particle Filter to Histogram. The original localization strategy was a Particle Filter: simulate thousands of possible robot positions, cull the ones that do not match sensor readings, and converge on the true position over time. In simulation it converged beautifully. On the physical robot, it failed catastrophically. Two of the four ToF sensors had a hardware anomaly: beyond their 1.2 m range, instead of returning an error signal, they returned a spurious reading of exactly 1 metre. The particle filter, seeing a consistent "1 m wall" reading, would localize the robot at a position matching that reading — completely wrong. Recovery was impossible. The algorithm had to be scrapped entirely mid-semester.

The replacement was a histogram-based localizer. Rather than reasoning about exact distances, the histogram only asked a binary question at each step: is there a wall in this direction or not? It maintained a probability distribution over all 1 ft x 1 ft maze cells and updated it based on the wall configuration around the robot at each step. This approach was robust to the ToF anomaly — a 1 m spurious reading simply became "no wall detected," not a localization catastrophe. It ran fast enough to update continuously without pausing the robot for seconds at a time. And it matched the natural discrete structure of the maze.

Block Centering — Solving Omni-Wheel Slip. Omni wheels are powerful but slippery. The original plan was to drive exactly one block-length (1 ft) between localization updates. In practice, slip meant the robot consistently overshot or undershot, causing the histogram to get out of sync with reality. The solution was a centering routine: using front and rear ToF readings, the robot computed its offset from the current cell centre using d_cen = L/2 - (min(ToF_f, ToF_b) mod L) and drove forward or backward until it was within tolerance of the centre. Localization updates only occurred at these confirmed centre points, effectively quantizing the robot's position into reliable discrete steps despite wheel slip.

Path Planning and Mission State Machine. Once localized, an A* planner generated one-block-at-a-time move commands from the current histogram cell to the target — first the loading zone, then one of four preprogrammed drop-off zones. The entire mission was managed as a layered state machine: alignment, exploration and localization, loading zone sequence (block detection, approach, grasp, fail-safe check, re-localization), and delivery. Obstacle avoidance ran continuously as a lower-priority safety layer underneath all higher-level states, able to override any planned command whenever a ToF threshold was violated.

Part 3 — Electrical Architecture

Wiring a Robot Brain Under Power, Timing, and Space Constraints

The electrical system centred on an Arduino Mega 2560 as the main microcontroller, chosen for its large number of GPIO pins required to drive four motors, poll multiple I2C ToF sensors, read IR sensors, control two servo channels for the gripper, and manage the limit switch — all simultaneously. Two L298N dual H-bridge motor driver boards controlled the four DC omni-wheel motors, with separate enable and direction pins for each motor allowing independent speed and direction control. A LM2596S buck converter stepped the 12V battery supply down to a regulated 5V rail for the microcontroller and sensors. An HC-05 Bluetooth module connected to the ESP32 on the laptop side for serial communication of sensor readings and drive commands.

One of the most significant electrical challenges was managing the shared I2C bus for the VL53L0X ToF sensors. Each sensor ships with the same default I2C address, requiring individual XSHUT pin sequencing at startup to assign unique addresses. Under heavy computation or motor current draw, the I2C bus would occasionally lock up, causing sensors to time out and return the anomalous 1 m readings that broke the particle filter. Power isolation between the motor drive rails and the sensor logic rails was imperfect, meaning motor current spikes could inject noise into sensor readings. A battery voltage display on the dashboard helped the team diagnose power-related failures during testing sessions.

Results and Reflection

What Worked, What Failed, and What We Would Do Differently

By Milestone 3, Tiny Tim achieved fully autonomous end-to-end operation: maze traversal, localization, block detection, pickup with fail-safe confirmation, and autonomous delivery to the correct drop-off zone. The strongest decisions were the shift to ToF sensing, histogram localization with block centering, and the fail-safe limit switch on the gripper. These three changes, each born out of a failure encountered during testing, made the difference between a robot that sometimes worked and one that was genuinely reliable.

The hardest lessons: omni wheels introduce slip that cannot be ignored in a localization system; a shared I2C sensor bus under motor load is a reliability liability; and a particle filter that works beautifully in simulation can be destroyed by a single hardware anomaly in the real world. For a next-generation design, the team identified LiDAR sensing, a hybrid Kalman Filter / histogram localizer, self-aligning gripper geometry, stepper motors with encoders, and isolated power rails as the highest-impact improvements. But as it stood, Tiny Tim — a laser-cut plywood and 3D-printed robot built in one semester — navigated a maze it had never seen, found a block it could not see from across the room, picked it up, and delivered it. That was enough.

Research Context

Overactive Bladder: A Widespread, Undertreated Condition

Overactive bladder (OAB) affects 18% of Canadian adults and up to 500 million people worldwide. The condition involves frequent and sudden urges to urinate caused by involuntary bladder muscle contractions, even when urine volume is low. It is particularly prevalent among adults over 65, and can cause anxiety, social withdrawal, depression, and dangerous falls. Current treatments have significant limitations: medications have side effects, surgical implantable devices are costly and invasive, and tibial nerve stimulation therapy requires monthly clinic visits.

The research team at UofT Institute of Biomaterials and Biomedical Engineering (IBBME), led by Professor Paul Yoo and Dr. Sasha John, identified a new neural target that could offer a more comfortable, home-based alternative to existing therapies.

The Research Question

Saphenous Nerve Stimulation: A More Sensitive Pathway

The team discovered that electrical stimulation of the saphenous nerve, a peripheral nerve in the leg, inhibits the bladder in a similar fashion to tibial nerve stimulation but at significantly lower amplitude. Because the saphenous nerve does not activate foot muscles the way the tibial nerve does, it is a more comfortable and versatile neural target. Pre-clinical studies confirmed that low-amplitude saphenous nerve stimulation could effectively suppress involuntary bladder contractions. This research was AGE-WELL funded and marks the first time the saphenous nerve had been targeted for OAB electrical stimulation therapy. A pilot clinical trial involving 30 patients was subsequently launched at Toronto Western Hospital, with participants self-administering 30 minutes of saphenous nerve stimulation daily over three months.

MATLAB Simulink Simulation

Building a Computational Model of the Urinary System

My primary contribution centred on developing a MATLAB Simulink simulation that replicates the dynamic behaviour of a functional urinary system. The model needed to capture the physiological interplay between bladder filling, detrusor muscle pressure, and the neural control mechanisms that govern normal voiding versus overactive contraction cycles. The goal was to provide a validated computational platform the research team could use to test stimulation protocols, predict patient responses, and interpret experimental data without relying exclusively on animal or human trials for every parameter sweep.

The Simulink model integrates multiple subsystems: a bladder volume dynamics block modelling fluid accumulation and pressure build-up, a detrusor muscle activation model capturing involuntary contraction behaviour characteristic of OAB, and a neural stimulation inhibition pathway representing the saphenous nerve reflex arc. MATLAB function blocks implement custom physiological equations derived from literature and experimental data. The model was validated against measured cystometrogram data from pre-clinical trials to ensure outputs matched real bladder pressure responses.

Pre-Clinical Work

In-Vivo Experiments and Dissection

Alongside the simulation work, I assisted with pre-clinical in-vivo experiments in the laboratory. These experiments involved surgically preparing animal subjects to allow real-time measurement of bladder pressure (cystometrogram recordings) while saphenous nerve stimulation was applied via fine electrode arrays. The experimental data provided the ground truth used to calibrate and validate the Simulink model, and deepened the understanding of the neural reflex pathways involved in bladder inhibition.

The lab work required real-time pressure signal acquisition and interpretation, and systematic variation of stimulation parameters -- frequency, amplitude, and pulse width -- to map the inhibitory response of the saphenous nerve pathway. These measurements directly informed both the clinical trial protocol and the physiological parameters used in the Simulink model.

Impact and Outcomes

Building the Evidence Base for a Novel Therapy

The Simulink model and pre-clinical experimental data contributed directly to the research team's ability to design and justify the clinical trial dosing protocol. The research programme successfully attracted interest from multiple industrial partners. If the clinical trial demonstrates efficacy, the saphenous nerve stimulation approach could offer a home-based, minimally invasive, self-administered therapy accessible to a large population of patients -- including the estimated 20-60% who fail to respond to existing treatments.

Project Context

A Real Engineering Brief from Magna International

Completed in collaboration with Magna International through MIE442 Machine Design at UofT. In a typical frameless door the glass is moved by a long routed steel cable. Over the vehicle life the cable stretches, pulleys wear, and temperature changes alter cable length -- if tension is not controlled the cable can jump grooves, the glass chatters or jams, and the regulator fails. Magna's existing tensioner meets performance targets but occupies valuable space and is approaching its packaging and durability limits.

The objective: develop, analyse, and validate a new constant-tensioner with fewer components while meeting all requirements. Maintain 40-75 N cable tension, withstand 710 N stall load, provide 25 mm compensation in discrete 1.5 mm steps, survive 50,000 cycles at SF above 1.25, manufacturable for automotive volume production.

Phase 1 -- Concept Generation

Four Mechanically Distinct SolidWorks Concepts

Functional Objectives and Constraints established covering tension reliability, automatic elongation compensation, compact door-module packaging, 710 N back-drive resistance, and 50,000 cycle service life. Four complete concepts developed and modelled to part level in SolidWorks.

Concept A -- Hook-Integrated Ratchet Drum. A rotating drum with torsion spring and ratchet-and-pawl. When slack develops the spring drives the drum to retract cable via an integrated hook, locking in 15 degree increments.

Concept B -- Gas Piston with Linear Ratchet. A compact gas piston provides near-constant tensile force. A circular pawl engages machined rod teeth locking in 1.5 mm steps (17 steps, 25.5 mm total).

Concept C -- Split Pulley Spring System. A pulley divided into two halves preloaded by springs. As cable tension drops, springs drive the halves apart, increasing effective pulley radius. Each half carries a pawl for irreversible incremental locking.

Concept D -- Spring Reel with Idler Pulley. A spring reel attached to a tether routed around an idler pulley. When cable loses tension the reel rotates and pulls the idler via the tether. A 20-tooth ratchet locks each 1.25 mm increment (25 mm total).

Phase 2 -- Synthesis and Concept Integration

From Four Candidates to One Design

Concept A established rotational spring torque as the most compact tensioning source. Concept B showed that large travel range fits in a small envelope when the ratchet is integrated into the moving element. Concept C identified that increasing cable path length geometrically avoids shock loading. Concept D reinforced that lever-arm conversion of spring torque gives excellent mechanical advantage throughout the stroke. Cable bending fatigue emerged as the shared vulnerability -- the final design uses a grooved lever to laterally deflect the cable rather than wrapping it, combining the spring-torque principle from A, path-extension from C, and compact ratchet locking from B.

Phase 3 -- Final Design

Lever-Based Constant Tensioner with Flat Coil Spring

A machined AISI 1018 steel lever pivots about a fixed pin on a baseplate inside the door. As the lever rotates clockwise driven by the flat coil spring unwinding, the cable guide pushes the cable sideways, increasing path length and restoring tension over 25 mm of travel. A ratchet integrated into the lever allows forward stepping via a torsion-spring-preloaded pawl, preventing back-drive. During stall events up to 710 N the ratchet and pawl carry the full stall load.

Phase 4 -- Critical Component Analysis

Force Analysis, Failure Modes, and Load Cases

LC1 normal operation (T = 40-75 N, N = 50,000 cycles): M = 7.59 N.m, spring force = 140.18 N. LC2 stall (T = 710 N, N = 5,000 cycles): pawl reaction = 1,685 N, pin resultant = 2,771 N. Fatigue: Modified Goodman with finite-life S-N. AISI 1018: Sut = 440 MPa, Sy = 370 MPa, 95% reliability.

ComponentLC1 SF (50k)LC2 SF (5k)FEA peak stressFEA SF
Lever Arm53583139 MPa2.66
Pivot Pin31.74.00289 MPa1.28 -- governing
Pawl--2.16141 MPa2.63
Phase 5 -- ANSYS FEA Validation

Mesh Convergence and Component Safety Margins

Phase 7 -- Tolerancing and Manufacturability

GD and T, Process Selection, and Design Optimisation

Lever arm: 4.00 mm pivot bore plus or minus 0.02 mm, 7.00 mm cable slot width plus or minus 0.02 mm. Pawl pivot hole plus or minus 0.02 mm. Pin diameter 4.00 plus or minus 0.02 mm. Baseplate and lever: 2.5-axis CNC milling. Flat coil spring: custom-ordered from 301 stainless strip steel. Lever arm and pawl thickness reduced after margins confirmed well above target. Cable groove radius widened to reduce stress concentration and accommodate cable positional variation.

Simulation Methodology

Coupled Transient Thermal and Static Structural

Two-stage ANSYS Workbench pipeline. Stage 1: 2.265-second Transient Thermal with 9,600 W heat flux on brake-pad faces, time-varying convection (60 W/m2K decaying to 0). Stage 2: Static Structural with imported temperature body load, 2.22 kN braking force, cylindrical supports at three mounting bolts. SS410: E=190 GPa, sigma_y=290 MPa, k=30 W/mK. Convergence to 1% per mesh refinement cycle.

Design Iterations

Exploring the Cutout Design Space

IterationVolumeSF (Static)SF (Thermal+Static)Status
CV12.74e-5 m30.168--Failed
CV24.72e-5 m30.030--Failed
CV33.81e-5 m31.3450.199Thermal critical
BaseRotor1,464 mm31.5520.440Passed
Prototyping

Physical Validation

FDM 3D-printed scaled mockups (PLA, 60% scale) validated assembly fit. Waterjet-cut aluminium 6061 coupons tested in the UofT MechE tensile rig empirically validated FEA failure modes.

Phase 1 -- Circuit Design and SPICE Simulation

Designing the Current Sense Stage from First Principles

Non-inverting summing amplifier with LM324N selected for single-supply capability and ground-referenced input, ideal for low-side sensing. Resistors 1k-100k standard 5%. LTspice confirmed the full 0.25-3.3 V output range before any hardware was touched.

Phase 2 -- PCB Layout in Eagle CAD

From Schematic to Manufacturable PCB

3rd-order Butterworth LPF added targeting -38 dB at 5 kHz. PCB routed in Eagle CAD with copper ground pour on both layers, short shunt-to-op-amp traces, and isolation of the high-current path from analog signals.

Phase 3 -- Assembly, Testing and Debugging

Soldering, Debugging, and Characterising the Real Board

PCBs hand-soldered at UofT lab sessions. Deviation above 1-2 A traced to cold solder joints, resistor thermal drift, and PCB parasitic capacitance. Oscilloscope confirmed filter working correctly at the current step.

Phase 4 -- Revised Design and Final Characterisation

A Corrected Board in a Complete Power Supply

Revised board integrated into benchtop power supply with LCD readout. Voltage accuracy within plus or minus 0.06 V. Current accuracy within plus or minus 0.02 A at mid-range targets.

Hardware Architecture

Wiring a Vision Pipeline Under Competition Pressure

Three interconnected hardware layers: ESP32-CAM served JPEG images over Wi-Fi, Arduino Uno R3 managed four tactile buttons via serial at 9600 baud, 16x2 I2C LCD displayed recognized text for users with partial vision.

Software Pipeline

From Photons to Words in Under Two Seconds

EasyOCR chosen over Tesseract for its deep-learning backbone (CRAFT + CRNN). Tokens fed a 30-word rolling memory buffer. pyttsx3 provided fully offline TTS. 200ms software debounce prevented serial stream corruption.

Results

24 Hours. One Shot. A Working Device.

Successfully read printed documents, handwritten notes, and signage. Advanced to the UTEK 2026 finalist presentation round. Future work: Raspberry Pi Zero 2W for untethered operation, multilingual EasyOCR, PETG chassis.
